---
title: "Big-O Notation"

---

<!-- # Big-O Notation -->

## What is Big-O?

Big-O tells us:

> “How fast or slow does a program become as data grows?”

Not with:

* 10 items
* 100 items

But with:

* 1 million
* 100 million
* billions

---

## Real-Life Analogy

Imagine searching for a name in:

### 1. Small notebook

Easy.

### 2. Huge phonebook

Now speed matters.

The way you search changes performance.

That’s what Big-O measures.

---

## Why AI Engineers Must Learn It

AI systems deal with:

* huge datasets
* embeddings
* vector databases
* streaming data
* retrieval systems
* inference requests

Even small inefficiencies become expensive.

Example:

* 1 ms × 1 request = fine
* 1 ms × 100 million requests = disaster

---

## The Main Big-O Types

---

## O(1) — Constant Time

### Meaning

Time does NOT grow with data size.

#### Example

Getting one item from an array.

```python
arr = [10, 20, 30, 40]

print(arr[2])
```

No matter:

* 4 items
* 4 million items

Access is instant.

---

### Real AI Example

Embedding lookup:

```python
embedding = embeddings[user_id]
```

Fast retrieval.

---

## O(n) — Linear Time

### Meaning

Work grows linearly with data.

More data → more work.

#### Example

```python
for x in arr:
    print(x)
```

If array size doubles,
work doubles.

---

### Real AI Example

Scanning all documents:

```python
for doc in documents:
    search(doc)
```

Common in:

* NLP preprocessing
* dataset cleaning
* brute-force search

---

## O(log n) — Logarithmic Time

### Meaning

Problem size reduces each step.

Very efficient.

---

### Real-Life Example

Guessing a number:

1–1000

You keep dividing range in half.

That’s binary search.

---

### Example

```python
binary_search(arr, target)
```

1 million items:

* linear search → 1,000,000 checks
* binary search → about 20 checks

Huge difference.

---

### Real AI Example

Used in:

* search systems
* databases
* indexing
* retrieval systems

---

## O(n log n)

Very common in efficient algorithms.

---

### Example

Efficient sorting:

```python
sorted(arr)
```

Uses optimized sorting internally.

---

### Real AI Example

Used in:

* ranking systems
* sorting recommendations
* preprocessing pipelines

---

## O(n²) — Quadratic Time

### Meaning

Every item compares with every other item.

Dangerous for large data.

---

### Example

```python
for i in arr:
    for j in arr:
        print(i, j)
```

10 items → 100 operations

1000 items → 1,000,000 operations

---

### Real AI Example

Similarity comparison:

```python
for user1 in users:
    for user2 in users:
        compare(user1, user2)
```

Very expensive.

This is why vector databases use ANN algorithms.

---

## O(2ⁿ) — Exponential Time

Extremely slow.

Usually brute force recursion.

---

### Example

```python
def fib(n):
    if n <= 1:
        return n
    return fib(n-1) + fib(n-2)
```

Looks simple.
Becomes terrible fast.

---

## Visualization

| Big-O      | Performance |
| ---------- | ----------- |
| O(1)       | Excellent   |
| O(log n)   | Very Fast   |
| O(n)       | Good        |
| O(n log n) | Acceptable  |
| O(n²)      | Slow        |
| O(2ⁿ)      | Very Slow   |

---

## Simple Way to Think About It

| Situation                          | Big-O      |
| ---------------------------------- | ---------- |
| One lookup                         | O(1)       |
| Scan everything                    | O(n)       |
| Keep dividing search space         | O(log n)   |
| Sort efficiently                   | O(n log n) |
| Compare everything with everything | O(n²)      |

---

## Practical AI Engineering Examples

| Task                          | Complexity |
| ----------------------------- | ---------- |
| HashMap lookup                | O(1)       |
| Scan dataset                  | O(n)       |
| Binary search in index        | O(log n)   |
| Sorting embeddings            | O(n log n) |
| Brute-force similarity search | O(n²)      |

---

## Important Mindset

Big-O is NOT:

* exact speed
* milliseconds
* hardware performance

It measures:

> growth of work as input grows

---

## Golden Rule

When data becomes massive:

* O(1)
* O(log n)
* O(n)

survive.

But:

* O(n²)
* O(2ⁿ)

become dangerous.

---
